name: Current Electricity
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Electric Current and Current Density
sections.body:
Electric current is the rate of flow of charge through a cross-section: $$I = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$$ SI unit: ampere (A) = C/s. Conventional current is in the direction of positive-charge flow (opposite to electron flow in a metal).

For uniform current through area $A$, the current density $\vec{j}$ has magnitude $j = I/A$ and points along $\vec{v}_d$ for positive carriers. In general $I = \int \vec{j}\cdot d\vec{A}$. Unit: A/m².

Current is a scalar (does not obey vector addition at junctions — Kirchhoff's current law applies), but current density is a vector.

sections.heading: 2. Drift Velocity and Mobility
sections.body:
In a conductor, free electrons collide randomly with ions; an applied field $\vec{E}$ superimposes a small drift velocity $\vec{v}_d$ opposite to $\vec{E}$: $$\vec{v}_d = -\dfrac{e\vec{E}}{m}\tau$$ where $\tau$ is the average time between collisions and $m$ the electron mass. Typical $v_d \sim$ mm/s.

Current in terms of drift: $I = neAv_d$, where $n$ is number density of free electrons. Hence $j = nev_d$.

Mobility is drift velocity per unit field: $\mu = v_d/E = e\tau/m$. Unit: m²/(V·s). Conductivity $\sigma = ne\mu$.

sections.heading: 3. Ohm's Law and Resistance
sections.body:
Ohm's law: at constant temperature, $V = IR$, where $R$ is the resistance of the conductor. Unit: ohm (Ω) = V/A.

In microscopic form: $\vec{j} = \sigma \vec{E}$, equivalently $\vec{E} = \rho\vec{j}$, where $\rho$ is resistivity and $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is conductivity.

For a uniform conductor of length $l$ and area $A$: $$R = \rho\dfrac{l}{A}$$ Resistivity $\rho$ is a material property, independent of dimensions. Substances obeying Ohm's law (linear $V$-$I$) are ohmic; semiconductors, vacuum tubes, etc., are non-ohmic.

sections.heading: 4. Temperature Dependence of Resistivity
sections.body:
For metals, resistivity increases with temperature (more frequent collisions): $$\rho_T = \rho_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$ where $\alpha$ is the temperature coefficient of resistivity. For typical metals $\alpha \approx 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ (positive).

For semiconductors and insulators, $\rho$ decreases with $T$ (more carriers thermally excited) — $\alpha$ is negative.

Some alloys (Manganin, Constantan) have very small $\alpha$ — used for standard resistors. Superconductors show $\rho = 0$ below a critical temperature.

sections.heading: 5. Combination of Resistors
sections.body:
Series: same current, voltages add. $$R_s = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots$$ Equivalent resistance is greater than the largest individual.

Parallel: same voltage, currents add. $$\dfrac{1}{R_p} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \cdots$$ Equivalent is less than the smallest individual.

For two resistors in parallel: $R_p = R_1 R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$.

sections.heading: 6. EMF, Internal Resistance, Terminal Voltage
sections.body:
EMF ($\varepsilon$) of a cell is the work done by its non-electrostatic source per unit positive charge in carrying it from negative to positive terminal inside the cell (open circuit).

A real cell has internal resistance $r$. When current $I$ flows: $$V = \varepsilon - Ir$$ where $V$ is the terminal voltage. On discharging, $V < \varepsilon$; on charging, $V = \varepsilon + Ir$.

Maximum (short-circuit) current: $I_{sc} = \varepsilon/r$.

For cells in series: $\varepsilon_{eq} = \sum \varepsilon_i$, $r_{eq} = \sum r_i$. For parallel cells of identical $\varepsilon$, $r$: $\varepsilon_{eq} = \varepsilon$, $r_{eq} = r/n$.

sections.heading: 7. Kirchhoff's Laws
sections.body:
Junction (KCL) rule: at any junction, $\sum I_{\text{in}} = \sum I_{\text{out}}$, i.e. $\sum I = 0$. Conservation of charge.

Loop (KVL) rule: around any closed loop, $\sum \Delta V = 0$. Conservation of energy. Sign convention: traversing a resistor in current direction gives $-IR$; traversing a cell from $-$ to $+$ gives $+\varepsilon$.

These rules let you solve any DC network with multiple sources and loops by setting up simultaneous equations.

sections.heading: 8. Wheatstone Bridge
sections.body:
Four resistors $P, Q, R, S$ form a quadrilateral with a galvanometer between two opposite junctions and a battery between the other two. The bridge is balanced (zero galvanometer current) when $$\dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{S}$$ At balance, the galvanometer arm carries no current, so its exact resistance doesn't matter. Used in the metre bridge to measure unknown resistances precisely.

The metre bridge applies the principle with $P, Q$ as resistance per unit length on a uniform wire: $R/S = l/(100-l)$ where $l$ cm is the balance length.

sections.heading: 9. Potentiometer
sections.body:
A long uniform wire of resistance per unit length $\sigma_R$ carries a constant current; potential drop is uniform along its length. The potential gradient $k = $ V per unit length.

Used to measure EMF (no current drawn at balance, so no $Ir$ drop). If unknown EMF $\varepsilon$ balances at length $l$: $\varepsilon = kl$.

To compare two EMFs: $\dfrac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2} = \dfrac{l_1}{l_2}$. To find internal resistance $r$ of a cell: balance EMF $\varepsilon$ at $l_1$ open-circuit, then with shunt $R$ across cell at $l_2$: $r = R(l_1 - l_2)/l_2$.

Potentiometer is preferred over voltmeter because it draws no current at balance — measures true EMF.

sections.heading: 10. Power and Heating Effect
sections.body:
Power dissipated in a resistor: $$P = VI = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$$ Unit: watt (W). Energy dissipated as heat in time $t$: $W = Pt$.

Joule's law of heating: $H = I^2 R t$ joules. This is the basis of fuses, heaters, bulbs.

For a battery delivering current $I$: total power $P_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon I$, power delivered to external circuit $P_{\text{ext}} = VI = \varepsilon I - I^2 r$.

Maximum power transfer occurs when external resistance equals internal resistance ($R = r$).

summary: Current $I = nev_d A$ from drift velocity. Ohm's law $V = IR$, with $R = \rho l/A$. Resistivity rises with $T$ in metals, falls in semiconductors. Real cells: $V = \varepsilon - Ir$. Kirchhoff's junction and loop rules solve networks. Wheatstone $P/Q = R/S$ enables precise resistance measurement. Potentiometer compares EMFs without drawing current. Power dissipated = $I^2 R$.
pitfalls: Drift velocity is tiny (mm/s); current signal travels at near-light speed because the field is set up nearly instantly throughout the wire.
EMF and terminal voltage differ by Ir; only equal when no current flows.
In a balanced Wheatstone bridge, galvanometer reading is zero — its exact resistance does NOT affect balance condition.
Resistors in parallel: 1/R_p formula, NOT R_1 R_2/(R_1+R_2) for more than two — many students misapply.
Internal resistance r is in series with the EMF inside the cell; on discharging V < ε, on charging V > ε.
Conventional current opposite to electron flow — confused frequently.
